\chapter{Boolean Analysis — Round Trip}
%%% generated by the blueprint pipeline from TCSlib.BooleanAnalysis.Switching.RoundTrip
\section{Overview}

This module proves the main round-trip property for the Razborov encoding used in the
switching lemma: decoding the encoding of a (bad) restriction recovers the original
restriction. From this round trip it follows that the encoding map is injective on bad
restrictions. The intermediate lemmas track invariants on the encoder/decoder state
(restrictions $\rho_0$ and $\sigma$) through the clause-processing fold.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Declarations}

\begin{lemma}[Free variable preserved by clause processing]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.pcl_none_implies_rho_free}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_ne_none}
If the restriction component $(\mathrm{processClauseLits}\ \mathit{lits}\ \mathit{path}\ \rho_0\ \sigma).2.1$
is unset at a variable $v$ (equal to \texttt{none}), then $\rho_0$ is already unset at $v$,
i.e.\ $\rho_0\,v = \texttt{none}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[First non-killed clause preserved under encoding]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.find_clause_preserved_in_encode}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, SwitchingLemma2.Literal.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Term.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_not_kills_first_clause, SwitchingLemma2.first_clause_preserved, Term}
For a DNF $f$ with no two literals in a term sharing a variable, suppose the encoder finds
$t_{\mathrm{clause}}$ as the first term not killed by $\rho_0$. Under the agreement
hypotheses relating $\rho_0$, $\sigma$, and the decoder restriction $\rho_{0,\mathrm{dec}}$
(agreeing with the encoder output on free variables and with $\rho_0$ on set variables),
the decoder restriction also finds $t_{\mathrm{clause}}$ as its first non-killed clause.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Filtered free-variable literals stay in the index list]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.mem_filter_freeVars_zipIdx}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars}
If a pair $p$ lies in the list obtained by filtering the indexed literals of
$t_{\mathrm{clause}}$ (its \texttt{zipIdx}) to those whose variable is free under $\rho_0$,
then $p$ is a member of $t_{\mathrm{clause}}.\mathrm{zipIdx}$ and its variable is free,
i.e.\ $\rho_0\,(p.1.\mathrm{var}) = \texttt{none}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[No aux entry targets a still-free variable]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_ne_of_pcl_none}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_ne_none, SwitchingLemma2.zipIdx_drop_spec, Term}
Assuming every literal in $\mathit{lits}$ is an indexed literal of the term $t$, if the
restriction component $(\mathrm{processClauseLits}\ \mathit{lits}\ \mathit{path}\ \rho_0\ \sigma).2.1$
is unset at $v$, then no auxiliary entry produced by the fold targets $v$: for every aux
entry $e$, dropping $e.1$ literals of $t$ to a head literal $l$ gives $l.\mathrm{var} \neq v$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Encoder first component agrees with recursive step]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_eq_rec}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.Term.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_acc, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, Term}
When the encoder finds $t_{\mathrm{clause}}$ as the first clause not killed by $\rho_0$ and
its filtered free-variable literals form a nonempty list $\mathit{fl}::\mathit{fls}$, the
first component of the encoder run with fuel $\mathrm{fuel}+1$ on path $\mathit{step}::\mathit{rest}$
equals the first component of the recursive encoder run with fuel $\mathrm{fuel}$ on the
processed state $\mathrm{pcl} = \mathrm{processClauseLits}\,(\mathit{fl}::\mathit{fls})\,(\mathit{step}::\mathit{rest})\,\rho_0\,\sigma$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Round-trip invariant for $\sigma$]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.roundtrip_inv_hC'}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.foldl_sigma_stable, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_ne_nonfree, Term}
Under the freeness and membership hypotheses on $\mathit{lits}$ and the agreement of
$\sigma_{\mathrm{dec}}$ with $\sigma$ on variables set by $\rho_0$, the $\sigma$-fold over
the aux entries (which clears $l.\mathrm{var}$ for each dropped head literal $l$) applied to
$\sigma_{\mathrm{dec}}$ agrees with $\sigma$ at every variable $v$ where the processed
restriction $(\mathrm{processClauseLits}\ \mathit{lits}\ \mathit{path}\ \rho_0\ \sigma).2.1$
is set.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Round-trip invariant for $\rho_0$]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.roundtrip_inv_hD'}
\leanok
\uses{Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.foldl_rho_stable, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_foldl_rho_eq_of_set, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_rho_stable, Term}
Under the freeness and membership hypotheses on $\mathit{lits}$ and the agreement of
$\rho_{0,\mathrm{dec}}$ with $\rho_0$ on variables set by $\rho_0$, the $\rho_0$-fold over
the aux entries (which sets $l.\mathrm{var}$ to $e.2$ for each dropped head literal $l$)
applied to $\rho_{0,\mathrm{dec}}$ agrees with the processed restriction
$(\mathrm{processClauseLits}\ \mathit{lits}\ \mathit{path}\ \rho_0\ \sigma).2.1$ at every
variable $v$ where that restriction is set.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Generalized go-level round-trip]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.go_roundtrip_gen}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.width, Literal, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.Term.killedBy, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_acc, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_sigma_indep_at_free, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_snd_sigma_indep, SwitchingLemma2.find_clause_preserved_in_encode, SwitchingLemma2.foldl_rho_stable, SwitchingLemma2.foldl_sigma_stable, SwitchingLemma2.go_roundtrip, SwitchingLemma2.mem_filter_freeVars_zipIdx, SwitchingLemma2.pcl_none_implies_rho_free, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_aux_ne_of_pcl_none, SwitchingLemma2.processClauseLits_sigma_none_of_rho_none, SwitchingLemma2.processEntries_of_processClauseLits, SwitchingLemma2.roundtrip_base, SwitchingLemma2.roundtrip_inv_hC', SwitchingLemma2.roundtrip_inv_hD', SwitchingLemma2.term_length_le_width}
The generalized round-trip statement: for a DNF $f$ of width at most $w$ with distinct
variables per term, given decoder states $\sigma_{\mathrm{dec}}$ and $\rho_{0,\mathrm{dec}}$
that satisfy the round-trip invariants relative to the encoder output and enough decoder
fuel, the first component of the decoder run on the encoder's output list equals the
original $\sigma$. This is the inductive core from which the closed round trip follows.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Go-level round-trip]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.go_roundtrip}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.width, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.encode_go_fst_nonfree, SwitchingLemma2.go_roundtrip_gen}
Specializing the generalized round trip to $\sigma = \rho_0 = \rho$: for a width-$w$ DNF
$f$ with distinct variables per term, running the encoder on $\rho$ and then running the
decoder (with one more than the encoded list length as fuel) on the encoder output recovers
$\rho$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Decode of encode recovers the restriction]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovDecode_encode}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.go_roundtrip, SwitchingLemma2.razborovDecode, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}
For a width-$w$ DNF $f$ with distinct variables per term and a bad restriction $\rho$ for
$f.\mathrm{eval}$ at depth $d$, decoding the Razborov encoding of $\rho$ returns $\rho$:
$\mathrm{razborovDecode}\ f\ w\ (\mathrm{razborovEncode}\ f\ w\ d\ \rho) = \rho$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[Injectivity of the Razborov encoding]
\lean{SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode_injective}
\leanok
\uses{DNF, DNF.eval, DNF.width, SwitchingLemma2.IsBadRestriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.razborovDecode_encode, SwitchingLemma2.razborovEncode}
The Razborov encoding is injective on bad restrictions: for a width-$w$ DNF $f$ with
distinct variables per term, if $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$ are both bad restrictions for
$f.\mathrm{eval}$ at depth $d$ and their encodings agree,
$\mathrm{razborovEncode}\ f\ w\ d\ \rho_1 = \mathrm{razborovEncode}\ f\ w\ d\ \rho_2$,
then $\rho_1 = \rho_2$.
\end{theorem}
